content.text: operator (logical AND) is the most concise way to render something conditionally in JSX. When the left side is truthy, React renders the right side. When it is falsy, React renders nothing — in theory. In practice, certain falsy values create a subtle bug that every React developer eventually hits: the zero renders as visible text.

attrs.alt: Two panels: left shows how JS && evaluates (falsy returns the condition value, truthy returns the right side); right shows pitfall values 0, empty string, and NaN with safe alternatives
attrs.title: How && Short-Circuits in JSX

content.text: does not return a boolean — it returns one of its operands. JavaScript evaluates the left side first. If it is falsy, the entire expression short-circuits and returns that falsy value unchanged. If it is truthy, the expression evaluates and returns the right side.

// JS returns the operand, not a boolean
false && 'hello'    // → false
null && 'hello'     // → null
undefined && 'hello' // → undefined
0 && 'hello'        // → 0       ← the dangerous one
'' && 'hello'       // → ''      ← also dangerous
NaN && 'hello'      // → NaN     ← also dangerous

true && 'hello'     // → 'hello'
1 && 'hello'        // → 'hello'
'hi' && 'hello'     // → 'hello'
as "render nothing." It does NOT treat
as "render nothing" — those are converted to text nodes in the DOM.

Test both the zero case and the truthy case explicitly — the zero pitfall passes TypeScript type checking and linting, so only a runtime test catches it:
TypeScript can catch the zero pitfall — but only if your type reflects that the value can be
. When a prop is typed as
warning in strict-mode projects, but not a hard error. The eslint-plugin-react rule
in your ESLint config turns the zero pitfall into a linting error that blocks CI — the most reliable way to prevent it from reaching production.
Conditionally appearing content can confuse screen readers if the DOM change is not announced. When
adds a banner, alert, or status message that the user must notice, pair it with an ARIA live region:
Without a live region, screen readers only announce content that gains focus. A banner that appears via
but has no focus management is invisible to assistive technology.
